speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 10, 1996. Our survivor is Shoshana Kahn. Her maiden name was Saks. I'm Lorrie Fein. We're in Brooklyn, New York, the United States of America, and our language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is June 10th, 1996. Our survivor is Shoshana Kahn. Her maiden name was Saks. I'm Lorrie Fein. We're in Brooklyn, New York, the United States of America, and our language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is-- that does it. You wait too long and start giggling.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Could you please tell us your name with the spelling?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoshana Kahn, S-H-O-S-H-A-N-A K-A-H-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saks, S-A-K-S. And my-- and I was called Rose, R-O-S-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Shoshana, S-H-O-S-H-A-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I had my-- I had a German, a German name-- is Victoria [? Rima. ?] And the French name, [? Jean Simone. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Have to. 30th of July, 1924.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 71, am I? Good gracious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Latvia. Libau, Liepaja, Libave. That's-- in that order, that is the German, the Latvian, and the Russian name for that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell the German and the Latvian?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-B-A-- Libau. L-I-B-A-U is Libau. And Liepaja, L-I-E-P-A-J-A. It's called now Liepaja.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: It comes, it-- the word comes-- Liepaja comes from the lindenbaum. The, the tree linden. Leipzig and Liepaja have the same etymological background. Very important. [LAUGHS] Lots of linden trees in, in Liepaja. That's why it's called Liepaja.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the town look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town is on the-- it was a lovely town. It was on the Baltic Sea. It, it-- summer which was used for people from tourism to take the bus and to spend the summer. The climate was extremely pleasant. We were not-- we had the tail of the gulf-- whatever that, that-- gulf stream. So it was not too cold winter and not so hot in summer. Always lovely. And fabulous beaches and wonderful parks and a lovely Jewish community-- about 12,000, 11,500, 12,000 Jews who usually were the doctors, the teachers, and the businesspeople. And they and the Baltic Germans used to socialize with the Latvians. We had very little fraternization. As-- as the joke goes, we talked Latvian to the dog and the, and the maid. Latvian didn't take it very gladly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Latvian language like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is-- well, it comes from Sanskrit. It is related to Lithuanian and Estonian. Very few people speak it. There have-- they had a few, a few reknowned poets were ladies. One was then was Aspazija. And that-- and the-- and they had very little literature. It was-- if one read a book written by Latvians, always looked as if, sounded as if they had copied it from some Russian. From Tolstoy or from Chekhov or something. Had the same ambiance. [SPEAKING LATVIAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Latvian. This is a little song like that. What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little kitten and a little, little puppy meet on the road and, and they are surprised. [SPEAKING LATVIAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so on and on and on. You mentioned the Jewish population of the area. What was the non-Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had the Germans and the Latvians. And some Russians. But it was-- Libau was a German town in culture. I mean, we were Germanophiles, all right. As in many of those small states, the Jews tried to identify with the higher culture. And the-- the-- in Libau, German was the culture. Anything which came from Germany, I mean, was 100% wonderful. The books, the music, the-- the clothes, the chocolates. Everything was perfect. The people who lived more on the eastern side of Latvia, they emulated the Russians, of course. And they talked Russian. And so we were raised in a German ambience and atmosphere. And, and in, in school-- even in the private school where I-- high school, the language we were taught was German. Well, we studied Latvian on the side like a foreign language. And that was very strange to my mind. Now in hindsight.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you would go out on the street and, and speak to natives, what language would you have used?

speaker: interviewee
text: Natives! We talked German. We didn't meet natives. I mean, natives? You mean Latvians? You--you would--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whoever you'd meet.

speaker: interviewee
text: You would-- the policeman would have been a Latvian. We knew Latvian. Till '35-- in '35 they, they-- we-- they became very nationalistic. We had a new president-- Karlis Ulmanis-- and he declared that it's going on. It-- this is too far. I mean, after all we are Latvia. TIll '35, in the name of the streets, every document was in three languages. Latvian, German, Russian. Now not exactly that order. But now in '35, Karlis Ulmanis declared it should all, all be Latvian. And he was quite right about it. And then we started to study in Latvian in school too. The Jews were rather prosperous-- middle class. And our cultural life usually was centered in summer around the Kurhaus garden, Kurhaus Park and the symphony. Everybody went to the symphony every night. And we paraded down the Kurhaus Prospect and everybody met everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the summers. In winter it was quite a sleepy little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had one theater. And every year the theater opened with Tchaikovsky. The ballet And of course, one went there. And the theater was well newly painted. Everything freshly painted. So when I, even now, hear the overture to, to Swan Lake, I smell the paint. That goes together. Da, da, da, da, da, da. And the smell of turpentine. They go together. [LAUGHS] And very little theater. I think the only play I saw was 'Lady-- Lady Windermere's Fan.' Fan, rather [INAUDIBLE] the word for it, yes. Excuse me. And that was in Latvian. So most of the stuff was ballet and opera and operetta.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your house look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small brick house. A two-family a house and had a biggish garden. That was about what I can tell you. It-- and it-- there were two houses on the, in the area. The front house and the back house. We lived in that smaller house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how big was this? How many rooms did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two bedrooms, a, a dining room, a so-called cabinet which my father had his books, kitchen, bathroom, that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us the address of the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. 46 Toma Iela, Toma Strasse, Toma Street. 46 Toma Street. Which was not far from the beach, not far from the park, and not far from anything because it was not the biggest town. It was the second biggest town in Latvia though.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Riga was the capital. Riga was the capital and was a glorious Hanseatic town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of Latvia was this town in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ours? We were on the west, on the west side of Latvia in, in an area called Kurzeme or Courland. The [GERMAN] in German. And that's where it was, on the beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your family? Could you start with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name is Chaim Saks. He was an accountant. He worked for a firm called [? Kiel. ?] He was a very gentle man-- a gentle man and a gentleman. And interested in the arts like all his brothers and father, and painted and played the violin and loved animals. He used to bring me mice as presents. And surprise, a mouse! And once now, this-- and he brought a little, he brought a little seal from the beach, the beach little baby seal. And we, we-- this town Latvia had a big harbor. And, and they brought the herring from Norway through our town to the west, to Germany, and all that. Had those big rafts. And sometimes those big rafts were-- they row those rafts toward the boats, and the rafts would break and spilled all the herring. And the whole town would smell of brine. But we had a raft like that. And there the little seal-- seal was there with the little ladder. And we had fish and he came out. Well, nobody really believed the story. When I later lived in Israel and told them a story which was not quite probable, they said, yes, and the seal was watching you. But there was a little seal till they took it back to the beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big did it get by the time he brought it back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could bring it back. It was not-- it was a small seal, a baby seal. And it could-- we brought him back to the beach and it swam off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, her name was Sophie. Her maiden name was Firkser. And she was a very resolute woman. And she was a horticulturist. Today you would call her that. She loved gardens. And she made any-- everything grow. I mean, the-- she-- everything grew. We had plants no end. And specialized in dahlias, which she kind of imported and bought from the Netherlands. And in winter, those tubers were living our-- under our beds. And my mom was a fabulous baker, a fabulous cook. And as it, and I, I understand her mother was not-- was a [? Balt. ?] And she was [INAUDIBLE]. And she had her-- she-- I think she will have depression. She was very often very sad or very up. But in those days, there was no Prozac and all that so we didn't quite know what was, what's the matter with Mommy. She just worried a lot. She used to come home. She was quiet sitting there or I came home and she was hopping around. A very attractive woman, very handsome. And she was the head of the family more or less, because her mom died very young. And she raised-- she was the oldest and raised the lot of the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- she worked-- my mom, yes, did work. My grandmother had a little factory. [LAUGHS] She made-- she made honey out of-- of sugar, which is artificial honey, and artificial goose fat. I mean, she, she boiled and boiled that stuff and added onions till it tasted like, like real goose fat. And, and they had it in a big tin with a goose on it and whatever, that I saw. And my mom helped her. And then during the-- in the Soviet Union when the Russians were the Soviet Union, my mom had a shop. And she sold groceries to the peasants you know, [? and grow ?] on the market. And she was a very good businesswoman. She was a very clever lady. Very, very energetic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a little sister. Her name was Miriam. She was 10 years younger than I, and a very sweet little kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I do. My grandma [? Lina. ?] She was my father's grandmother and she was an extraordinary woman. [LAUGHS] My, my-- this-- she was the black sheep of the family, [INAUDIBLE] in the family. She liked to get married often. And to, to wealthy gentlemen who have houses, preferably. And so I-- she had a few husbands and a few houses after my grandpa died. And she was always fun, always in a good mood. And I could always get what I wanted from, from, from Grandma. She had this lovely little house not far from the harbor with a big garden and a big yard where she used to dry fish on a, on a washing line. And all the flounders were hanging on there with the pins on the washing line, dripping. And the flies were humming. It was quite a sight. Yes, my Grandma [? Lina-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: At first--

speaker: interviewer
text: The first one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first was Saks. Then I think it was [? Gamsa ?] and I think it was [? Schtusser. ?] And I don't know what another-- must have been another gentleman. Everybody was aghast. I mean, those days a grandmother who marries a lot, why, it's not the usual norm of grandmothers. My sexy bubbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what she was. Lovely lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any other grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember my mother's father, whose was name was Hirsch. He was a handsome old man with a mustache. And there was something wrong with him mentally, I think. He-- I never remember him talking to me at all. He was just sitting in front of a desk with an old newspaper in front of him pretending to read. It was always the same newspaper for years and years and years and years. Very-- but he was handsome with those, that mustache, mustache. And that was my other grandfather. He lived with my Tante [? Minna ?] at the house, and Tante [? Johanna ?] and all those people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about any of your aunts or uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for my mother's side, there were three sisters. There was Tante-- Aunt Paula. She wore a [INAUDIBLE] and she lived in Danzig and she came to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she always thought I'm not good enough for her. What-- I had to write her. I'd write her letters on a, on a monthly basis. And what does one write one's aunt? So I wrote her about the beach and how the dunes look and how the water is and all that. And it-- it sounded awfully good. My aunt writes back to my mom that I am plagiarizing somebody! And I should be punished for, for copying letters and sending them to her! [LAUGHS] Which I never forgot, because I really wrote her nice letters

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, then there was Tante [? Johanna, ?] who was, who had scarlatina as a child and, and was quite, she had schizophrenic. And, and up in-- most of the time was in institutionalized. There was Uncle-- Uncle [? Boris, ?] the only brother. Who-- [LAUGHS] who, who one day announced Being Jewish is dangerous. [INAUDIBLE] turned into a Dane. Married a Dane, left for Denmark, and was never heard of again.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was before the-- before '39. And my mom never-- didn't want to forgive him. But during the war when things-- when the Germans marched in and things were really horrible, she said, Well, Boris was very clever to become a Dane. And from then on, I had this image being a Dane is the most wonderful thing to be. And I think I told it to you when I came to America first time, my mother-in-law [? Erna ?] took me to see And high and below, they were all saying, There is nothing like a Dane. And I was not at-- not surprised at all. Not surprised at all. There is nothing like a Dane. And well, there was-- and then there was my beloved aunt, Tante Minna. She was glorious, beautiful, talented, avante garde, interesting, charming, and lucky. She had-- she was very talented. She had this lovely salon where she, where she sewed the clothes-- dresses. In those days, people didn't buy-- well, people who are somebody wouldn't buy clothes from the rack. They went to a seamstress, to a salon, and sat in the waiting room and looked at the magazines and picked something out and went to the shops and got the material, came to fittings, and et cetera, et cetera. My aunt was very good at it. She was one of the top. Libau was a town where there were many Jewish people who were in that business. There were the people who did the coats. There's the people who did the costumes, the people who did the dresses and on and on. And for the children were other people. My aunt-- and they come even from Europe, from Lithuania, from Germany, and from other people just to Tante Minna and have their wardrobe freshed-- refreshed. And she was very successful at that. And I liked her a lot. We had-- we, she-- with her, one could talk about anything and everything. She was a lovely lady. And she had lots of gentlemen callers, as, as Tennessee Williams would have said. But they were only-- they were affairs, but never married her because of her handicap. She had only one leg. She-- her leg was amputated. And then that was-- after those gentle-- they broke up and they married somebody else, my aunt was extremely unhappy. I do remember that. Was very, very unhappy. And then she found somebody else. And so on and on and on. She-- she was so beautiful. I mean, everybody loved her. It was-- and, and not-- beautiful and interesting. A real Renaissance woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your daily life?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child or as a student?

speaker: interviewer
text: As a, as a young person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lived in Latvia. I mean, Libau. That was beach life. In winter, we're going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Going to school was great. We-- I went to a very lovely school. It was [? Heides-- ?] [? Heides ?] Private School. It was a private school. It was not a state school. And for Jewish children. And we were taught in German. And well, there were many Baltic German teachers among the teachers. We-- what was lovely about that school was I thought that we started in kindergarten, the same class was together, together, together till high school. I mean, we became like brothers and sisters. We were like an extended, extended family. I mean, should I meet somebody who was in school, we had so much in common. Remember the-- the teachers. And we were like brothers and sisters, really. It was very-- a very close relationship. And our class [INAUDIBLE], our class mother, we would call it, was for Dr.-- Mrs.-- Dr., for Dr. [? Weinright. ?] We called her Tante [? Push. ?] And she went with us, again, from beginning to end, and so on, on and on and on. The my-- my first teacher was a-- a wonderful teacher. It was in preschool. Her name was Mrs. Herma-- Fraulein Herma. Miss Herma. And I remember a very, very-- [BACKROUND NOISE] I remember very well because I was always such a big child. I was bigger than any other child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the parents came to pick up their children, they used to compare, yes? And here I came, that big slob, and says, now which grade are you? And I said, I'm in the four class. This is pre-school. And they used to stare at me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Fraulein Herma used to come running. 'Yes, she is in pre-school, but she's still young. Still young.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [LAUGHS] the next time somebody asked me, which grade are you, my little-- my child? I said, I am in pre-school, but I am young! [LAUGHS] And that sort of caused a lot of amusement.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know when I said it, it was-- it was funny. When the teacher said it, was accepted very easily. [INAUDIBLE] So I was still young. I would like to stay that way. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What subjects do you remember from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We studied? Languages. Quite a few. We had, first of all, German as, as a, as a language. German literature. We did Latvian as a language with literature. We had Hebrew, and later Yiddish. During the Soviet Union. They made us study Yiddish. Hebrew was out because it was fascistic and Zionistic. Yes. And we could-- had a choice between Latin-- Latin, French, or English. And Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what did you choose?

speaker: interviewee
text: English. Well, I life-- I loved magazine. I had to-- I, I adored magazine. I thought America is absolutely the thing. And I loved American movies. But the, the story was that we could not go to the movies, to every movie, till we were 18 years old, or 17 or something. So we were restricted to stupid children movies. We saw, for example, That we could see. And that was in [INAUDIBLE]. And we saw something-- something about Johann Strauss. That we could see. I mean, anything more risque, if we were not 17 or 18, we couldn't see. But I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made that rule?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that was a rule. We-- you had to be a certain age to see adult movies. I mean, what they called adult movies. Was nothing adult them them. I mean, they kissed or what. And yes, I saw maybe every week or every day after school. I and my, my little friend [? Ozzy, ?] we went to the movies to see

speaker: interviewer
text: And they talked all German. All the dwarves talked German. Grouchy said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] when he got angry. [LAUGHS] Yeah. And what else is amusing about Latvia, about Libau? The school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The school. Well, at the school, we had, we had quite a few German. We had Fraulein Nauthing and [? Adolf ?] Nauthing. They were the two Baltic teachers. She did, she did zoology and he did history. And she had blue hair and a blue nose to go with it. Yes, she was a, a spinster. [LAUGHS] And then there was Fraulein Gephardt. We did-- she did German. And she picked me always to do the reciting of the poems. And once I-- there was a poem, a poem about teensy little [INAUDIBLE] flowers and teensy little birdies and all that. And I resisted and said, I'm not, not reciting that one. I don't like it. And that was unheard of. So I got expelled for three days for, from school for resisting Fraulein Gephardt. She broke into tears, poor thing. And we had a very wonderful, a very extraordinary teacher, Professor [? Lang, ?] who did maths. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mrs. [? Soliviova, ?] who did, who did Russian with us. Fraulein Bern-Bernais, who was our exercise teacher, whatever you call that now. But she had an affair with the rabbi, and she was German. The rabbi had affair with her. [LAUGHS] He was a young rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that news?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody knew that. Fraulein Bern-Bernais. I was very good at those things. I always got high marks in those exercises. Because I was so big, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your friends from school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], definitely, I do. I mean, we were sitting in alphabetical order. So everybody with an S sat together. [LAUGHS] More or less. Oh, and then the big children sat in the back. So probably I was in the back next to, next to Hilda Saks, who was a, was a pianist and my-- a little-- little related to me. And she played the piano beautifully and had a big bosom at the age of 12. And never-- I, I was so envious. I cannot even tell you. Every time she played 'The Hungarian Rhapsody' and she [INAUDIBLE] those, those chords, her bosom was swaying! Oh! And all the boys were-- were standing around her. And they became such, such music lovers! She was a very good pianist. She was very lucky girl. Her father lived in South Africa. And the family lived in Libau. And once a year, or once-- he came to visit. And when he came to visit, of course they could have-- Marcie could have, or Hilda could have, anything she wanted. I, I said anything she wanted. We'd say, now you two girls go to the movies. That was a big deal. Not only go to the movies. Here is money, go and get yourself a cheesecake. So we can go to the movies and a cheesecake. But my parents would never. They would say either the movie or a cheesecake.

speaker: interviewer
text: So having a father in South Africa was extremely romantic and very, very good. And that besides, she got out with the last ship of Europe. [BACKROUND NOISE] She and her family. And to South Africa, and they were saved. How would you spend your free time with friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: My free time, be on the bike. Bikes, bicycling. Biking about the town and biking out of the town to [? Banadan, ?] to the woods, collecting mushrooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: The woods are full of mushrooms. We had chanterelle no end. And you know chanterelles, those little yellow ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: And berries, blueberries. And [INAUDIBLE], little wild strawberries. And we used to, in the evening, go run along the beach and collect amber. We read a lot. We discussed a lot. And we listened to a lot of music. We were quite an intellectual bunch, I would think, looking back to it. There was not a-- we didn't do the necking and, and, and all the way and whatever bit what I hear now. That was out of the question. I mean, if you had a, a boyfriend, we did homework together. We went to the movies together. [? Ozzy ?] used to cook for me sometimes. [? Ozzy ?] was a good cook. It was rather a nice situation. When I was-- when I was sick from school and stayed home, they came and brought homework and, and helped one. [? Ozzy ?] helped me with all my homework because he was a genius. He was very clever. And I, I never understood the trigonometry and geometry. But 'cause I was [? Ozzy's ?] friend-- [? Zelkin's ?] friend-- that Professor [? Lang ?] said, I will not have you fail. Anybody who's [? Zelkin's ?] friend will not fail in maths. Here you go again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Did you have any religious education?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some religious education in school. But it was extremely-- it was not taken seriously. The poor rabbi had-- didn't have a good time with us. Yes. We were not very respectful. I don't think we were. But we, I-- we, we-- my parents did the usual holiday thing. We had Pesach. We went-- we had it together with our neighbors, with the Edelsteins, who were in the [INAUDIBLE]. And there was this old grandpa with a beautiful red velvet hat, embroidered. He sat in a [INAUDIBLE]. And he brought the fish. And they had the lemonade, because of course, they had the lemonade factory. And I was sitting with a back to the kitchen because I was the youngest and I had to say the, the [? koshiot. ?] You know, the-- of course. And then when something was missing on the table, they sent me out to the cold kitchen to bring in. In those days, they had only the room heated we were in. And I was quite sure that the Eliyahu Hanavi was, was lurking in the shadows and ready to pounce on me. I was very scary. Besides our Hagganah had on the back the pictures of the Marranos. I mean, the Jews sitting-- [INAUDIBLE] sitting, having Seder in-- in a cellar, someone in hiding. And you see those guys coming in with spears and taking them away. And I had this terrible sense of fore-- premonition that something evil is going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the whole Seder thing was not very pleasurable for me. I mean, they killed little firstborns. I was the firstborn. Although it said it's boys, I wasn't quite sure they will not make a mistake.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eliyahu Hanavi was lurking in the shadows. And then we had mead to drink, which bubbled. And I saw him drinking. And he wore a toga. The [INAUDIBLE] Eliyahu Hanavi. I saw him in a white toga. And there's a picture of the Marranos being, being arrested. It was very scary. It was a scary occasion to me. I was a probably very sensitive child. [LAUGHS] Yes. I had this feeling that it's no, it's no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the end of our tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of our tape.
